buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

prices = pd.read_csv('multi_asset_prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Compute correlation on raw prices — non-stationary
corr_matrix = prices.corr()

print('Asset correlations:')
print(corr_matrix)
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

prices = pd.read_csv('multi_asset_prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Compute correlation on returns — stationary
returns = prices.pct_change().dropna()
corr_matrix = returns.corr()

print('Asset return correlations:')
print(corr_matrix)
description: Correlation matrix computed on raw prices instead of returns.
explanation: Raw price series are non-stationary — they have trends and changing means over time. Computing correlation on non-stationary data almost always produces spuriously high correlations (two trending assets appear highly correlated even if unrelated). Portfolio optimization and risk models built on price correlations will be completely wrong.
suggested_fix: Always compute correlation on returns (pct_change()) or log returns, not raw prices.

issues.title: Correlation computed on raw prices instead of returns
